comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the availability and impact of Starlink internet service on flights, with users sharing personal experiences, technical insights, and opinions on satellite internet versus traditional connectivity. Some express enthusiasm for the technology's potential, while others critique its cost, complexity, and broader implications for rural internet access.

comments.author: neilsharma425
comments.text:
Neat problem to work on. The tail number lookup is the hard part and it sounds like you solved it the right way, by finding the people who actually track this obsessively rather than trying to scrape it yourself.

Two questions: how stale does the tail assignment data get in practice, and do you have a way to detect when an enthusiast spreadsheet goes unmaintained? And what happens to your probability estimate when an airline swaps aircraft last minute, which seems to happen pretty often on regional routes?
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and knowledgeable perspective on the technical challenge of tracking aircraft and data reliability, showing understanding of the problem and solution.

comments.author: gpt5
comments.text: One nice thing about Starlink is that they force the airlines to offer it for free. I’m not sure why SpaceX is doing this, but it was surprising enough to me that my international WiFi was not only fast, but completely free that I researched it.
comments.analysis: The comment shares a personal observation about Starlink's pricing model on flights, which aligns with known promotional strategies, though the reasoning behind it is speculative.

comments.author: devin
comments.text: I've written it elsewhere, but: it is such a shame that the United States saw fit to run electricity _everywhere_, no matter how rural your location, but instead of do the same for rural internet we had to wait for... a private company to launch a global network of satellites. Yes, this post is about internet access while traveling 500mph, which is a different problem, but it is so messed up that people fall over themselves about Starlink for rural connectivity when it is an incredibly complex and expensive technology with huge ongoing costs that could have been solved once and for all by simply running some wires.
comments.analysis: The comment expresses a valid critique of infrastructure priorities, highlighting the complexity and cost of satellite internet versus wired solutions, though it simplifies some policy considerations.

comments.author: rayiner
comments.text: I tried Starlink on a United flight the other day (short hop from Hilton Head to DC) and it was amazing.
comments.analysis: A straightforward user experience report that supports the claim of Starlink's effectiveness on flights, though anecdotal and limited in scope.

comments.author: apitman
comments.text: I've only had it once, but inflight Starlink is a game changer. I was able to play a ranked AoE2 game over the Pacific Ocean.
comments.analysis: An enthusiastic personal account emphasizing low latency and usability of Starlink in flight, which is plausible given the technology's capabilities.

comments.author: ValentineC
comments.text:
As someone who's really not a fan of fElon (he made Twitter steal my OG username), it's nice to see people misuse the Starlink term, and I hope it would eventually be genericised [1]. ;)

The proper term should be Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellites, and there are other providers like Amazon [2] and Panasonic Avionics [3] that I hope other airlines would do business with.

[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Generic_trademark

[2] https://leo.amazon.com/

[3] https://www.panasonic.aero/blog/blog-post/what-is-low-earth-...
comments.analysis: This comment correctly clarifies terminology and broadens the perspective by mentioning other satellite internet providers, adding valuable context.

comments.author: nomilk
comments.text: It would be great to make this data into a browser extension that overlays the info when using Google Flights
comments.analysis: A practical and constructive suggestion to improve user experience by integrating Starlink availability data with popular flight booking tools.

comments.author: torcete
comments.text: People are so rude with their phones that I fear that starlink becomes popular in all flights.
comments.analysis: An emotional comment expressing social frustration rather than contributing meaningful insight about the technology or its impact.

comments.author: p0w3n3d
comments.text: For God's sake you can take 2-4 hours of not working, right? Just sit and relax, or take an audiobook with you! Or watch a cringy show from 90s. You don't have obligations of sharing #airplane #boeing #starlink #momwithbaby[kl]ickingmyseat every 5 minutes or so
comments.analysis: A dismissive comment that does not engage with the technical or practical aspects of inflight internet, offering little constructive value.

comments.author: Bombthecat
comments.text:
My trip from USA to Amsterdam doesn't have starlink, at all. Not a single plane. No matter the company.

So sad
comments.analysis: A personal observation about lack of Starlink availability on a specific route, which is plausible but anecdotal and limited in scope.
